title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 12

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of Cahokia, North America, c. 1200: “Large earthen mounds, plazas, wooden palisades, and nearby maize fields suggest a central place where leaders organized ceremonies, storage, defense, and regional gatherings.” What is the best interpretation of Cahokia supported by the description?

choices.A: Cahokia was an urban ceremonial and political center supported by agriculture.
choices.B: Cahokia was founded by European missionaries.
choices.C: Cahokia lacked social organization before 1450.
choices.D: Cahokia depended on Atlantic trade for maize.

questions.correctAnswerText: Cahokia was an urban ceremonial and political center supported by agriculture.
questions.explanation: The description links mounds, plazas, leaders, storage, defense, gatherings, and maize fields.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of Cahokia, North America, c. 1200: “Large earthen mounds, plazas, wooden palisades, and nearby maize fields suggest a central place where leaders organized ceremonies, storage, defense, and regional gatherings.” Which phrase best supports the claim that agriculture helped sustain Cahokia?

choices.A: “Wooden palisades”
choices.B: “Nearby maize fields”
choices.C: “Regional gatherings”
choices.D: “Large earthen mounds”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Nearby maize fields”
questions.explanation: Maize fields directly support the claim that agriculture helped sustain the center.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of Cahokia, North America, c. 1200: “Large earthen mounds, plazas, wooden palisades, and nearby maize fields suggest a central place where leaders organized ceremonies, storage, defense, and regional gatherings.” Which material feature most directly indicates ceremonial or public political activity?

choices.A: Maize fields
choices.B: Wooden palisades
choices.C: Plazas
choices.D: Nearby villages

questions.correctAnswerText: Plazas
questions.explanation: Plazas indicate spaces for public gathering, ceremony, and political display.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of Cahokia, North America, c. 1200: “Large earthen mounds, plazas, wooden palisades, and nearby maize fields suggest a central place where leaders organized ceremonies, storage, defense, and regional gatherings.” The description most directly challenges which misconception?

choices.A: That pre-1450 North American societies could develop complex centers without European rule
choices.B: That agriculture could support population concentration and political authority
choices.C: That public architecture could reflect social hierarchy
choices.D: That North American societies before 1450 were necessarily small, scattered, and politically simple

questions.correctAnswerText: That North American societies before 1450 were necessarily small, scattered, and politically simple
questions.explanation: Cahokia’s mounds, plazas, defenses, leaders, and agricultural base show complex organization.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of Cahokia, North America, c. 1200: “Large earthen mounds, plazas, wooden palisades, and nearby maize fields suggest a central place where leaders organized ceremonies, storage, defense, and regional gatherings.” Which broader context best explains the development of such a center?

choices.A: The decline of all agriculture in the Americas after 1200
choices.B: The arrival of European horses in North America before 1200
choices.C: The spread of maize agriculture and regional exchange in parts of North America
choices.D: The creation of industrial factory towns in the Mississippi Valley

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of maize agriculture and regional exchange in parts of North America
questions.explanation: Maize agriculture and exchange helped support larger ceremonial and political centers.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mamluk commercial court record, Cairo, c. 1340: “A merchant from Aden deposits pepper and indigo with a broker near the caravanserai. A judge records the partnership shares, and port agents report that ships from the Red Sea have arrived with Indian cloth.” What was the most likely purpose of the record?

choices.A: To describe commercial partnerships and legal oversight in a major Afro-Eurasian trade hub
choices.B: To abolish all Red Sea commerce
choices.C: To prove that Cairo was isolated from Indian Ocean trade
choices.D: To replace merchant partnerships with industrial banks

questions.correctAnswerText: To describe commercial partnerships and legal oversight in a major Afro-Eurasian trade hub
questions.explanation: The record includes merchants, brokers, partnership shares, judges, ports, and traded goods.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mamluk commercial court record, Cairo, c. 1340: “A merchant from Aden deposits pepper and indigo with a broker near the caravanserai. A judge records the partnership shares, and port agents report that ships from the Red Sea have arrived with Indian cloth.” Which phrase best supports the claim that legal institutions helped regulate trade?

choices.A: “Indian cloth”
choices.B: “A merchant from Aden”
choices.C: “A judge records the partnership shares”
choices.D: “Ships from the Red Sea”

questions.correctAnswerText: “A judge records the partnership shares”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows legal institutions recording and regulating commercial arrangements.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mamluk commercial court record, Cairo, c. 1340: “A merchant from Aden deposits pepper and indigo with a broker near the caravanserai. A judge records the partnership shares, and port agents report that ships from the Red Sea have arrived with Indian cloth.” Which broader context best explains the goods and places mentioned?

choices.A: Cairo linked Mediterranean, Red Sea, and Indian Ocean commercial circuits.
choices.B: Red Sea trade ended after the rise of the Mamluks.
choices.C: Indian cloth reached Cairo only after steamships appeared.
choices.D: Aden was disconnected from Afro-Eurasian commerce.

questions.correctAnswerText: Cairo linked Mediterranean, Red Sea, and Indian Ocean commercial circuits.
questions.explanation: Aden, Red Sea ships, Indian cloth, Cairo, brokers, and caravanserais show Cairo’s role as a trade connector.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mamluk commercial court record, Cairo, c. 1340: “A merchant from Aden deposits pepper and indigo with a broker near the caravanserai. A judge records the partnership shares, and port agents report that ships from the Red Sea have arrived with Indian cloth.” The record most directly illustrates which feature of long-distance trade?

choices.A: Merchants avoided brokers and legal records.
choices.B: Trade networks depended only on direct barter between producers.
choices.C: Commercial exchange could rely on brokers, courts, ports, and credit arrangements.
choices.D: Religious courts prevented all foreign merchants from trading.

questions.correctAnswerText: Commercial exchange could rely on brokers, courts, ports, and credit arrangements.
questions.explanation: The record shows brokers, judges, partnership shares, port agents, and imported goods.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Mamluk trade routes c. 1340 shows caravans from Cairo to Damascus, ships from Aden to Jeddah, and routes from Alexandria to Mediterranean ports. Labels identify pepper, indigo, Indian cloth, glassware, and metal goods.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Mamluk Egypt connected overland, Red Sea, and Mediterranean routes into a wider trading system.
choices.B: Cairo’s merchants traded only within Egypt.
choices.C: Indian Ocean goods never entered Mediterranean markets.
choices.D: Mediterranean routes replaced all caravan routes before 1200.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mamluk Egypt connected overland, Red Sea, and Mediterranean routes into a wider trading system.
questions.explanation: The map links Cairo, Damascus, Aden, Jeddah, Alexandria, Mediterranean ports, and Indian Ocean goods.

questions.prompt: Originalized Kanem-Bornu royal letter, Lake Chad region, c. 1570: “The mai commands that cavalry horses be counted and that scholars in the capital teach the law. Salt from the desert and grain from the villages shall be recorded, and raiders who disturb the caravan roads shall be punished.” What was the most likely purpose of the letter?

choices.A: To coordinate military power, Islamic learning, taxation, and protection of trade routes
choices.B: To abolish caravan trade in the Lake Chad region
choices.C: To reject the use of cavalry in state defense
choices.D: To replace Islamic scholars with European colonial officers

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate military power, Islamic learning, taxation, and protection of trade routes
questions.explanation: The letter references cavalry, scholars, law, salt, grain, records, caravan roads, and punishment of raiders.

questions.prompt: Originalized Kanem-Bornu royal letter, Lake Chad region, c. 1570: “The mai commands that cavalry horses be counted and that scholars in the capital teach the law. Salt from the desert and grain from the villages shall be recorded, and raiders who disturb the caravan roads shall be punished.” Which phrase best supports the claim that Islamic institutions contributed to state authority?

choices.A: “Salt from the desert”
choices.B: “Scholars in the capital teach the law”
choices.C: “Cavalry horses”
choices.D: “Grain from the villages”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Scholars in the capital teach the law”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows Islamic learning and legal authority supporting the state.

questions.prompt: Originalized Kanem-Bornu royal letter, Lake Chad region, c. 1570: “The mai commands that cavalry horses be counted and that scholars in the capital teach the law. Salt from the desert and grain from the villages shall be recorded, and raiders who disturb the caravan roads shall be punished.” Which broader environmental context best explains the mention of salt and grain?

choices.A: The state connected Saharan and Sahelian ecological zones through trade and taxation.
choices.B: Lake Chad states depended only on Atlantic plantation crops.
choices.C: Desert salt and village grain had no role in regional exchange.
choices.D: Grain agriculture disappeared from the Sahel after 1450.

questions.correctAnswerText: The state connected Saharan and Sahelian ecological zones through trade and taxation.
questions.explanation: Desert salt and village grain show exchange across different ecological zones.

questions.prompt: Originalized Kanem-Bornu royal letter, Lake Chad region, c. 1570: “The mai commands that cavalry horses be counted and that scholars in the capital teach the law. Salt from the desert and grain from the villages shall be recorded, and raiders who disturb the caravan roads shall be punished.” Which argument is best supported by the letter?

choices.A: Kanem-Bornu state power linked military organization, religious authority, and control of regional exchange.
choices.B: The mai rejected taxation as a source of authority.
choices.C: Caravan roads weakened all state-building in the Sahel.
choices.D: Islamic learning was unrelated to political authority in the Lake Chad region.

questions.correctAnswerText: Kanem-Bornu state power linked military organization, religious authority, and control of regional exchange.
questions.explanation: Cavalry, scholars, law, recorded goods, and protected caravan roads all support this argument.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Lê dynasty in Đại Việt, c. 1500–1600, shows provincial schools, Confucian examination sites, military colonies along the southern frontier, and rice-growing districts connected to the capital by river routes.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Đại Việt used education, frontier settlement, military organization, and agrarian administration to strengthen state authority.
choices.B: Đại Việt rejected Confucian education after 1450.
choices.C: The southern frontier had no connection to state expansion.
choices.D: Rice-growing districts were unrelated to political authority.

questions.correctAnswerText: Đại Việt used education, frontier settlement, military organization, and agrarian administration to strengthen state authority.
questions.explanation: The map shows schools, examination sites, military colonies, rice districts, river routes, and the capital.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Lê dynasty in Đại Việt, c. 1500–1600, shows provincial schools, Confucian examination sites, military colonies along the southern frontier, and rice-growing districts connected to the capital by river routes.” Which map feature best supports the claim that Confucian learning shaped governance?

choices.A: “River routes”
choices.B: “Military colonies”
choices.C: “Provincial schools”
choices.D: “Rice-growing districts”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Provincial schools”
questions.explanation: Provincial schools directly show the role of Confucian learning in governance.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Lê dynasty in Đại Việt, c. 1500–1600, shows provincial schools, Confucian examination sites, military colonies along the southern frontier, and rice-growing districts connected to the capital by river routes.” The pattern shown most directly reflects which broader process?

choices.A: The combination of bureaucratic education and frontier expansion in Southeast Asian state-building
choices.B: The replacement of all agriculture by nomadic pastoralism
choices.C: The collapse of state authority in mainland Southeast Asia
choices.D: The spread of Atlantic plantation slavery into Vietnam

questions.correctAnswerText: The combination of bureaucratic education and frontier expansion in Southeast Asian state-building
questions.explanation: Schools, examinations, frontier colonies, and rice districts show education and expansion working together.

questions.prompt: Originalized Pueblo testimony, New Mexico, c. 1680: “The friars burned our sacred things and ordered us to labor for their churches. Messengers carried knotted cords between villages so that we would rise together and drive out those who mocked our ancestors.” What was the most likely purpose of the testimony?

choices.A: To defend Spanish missionary authority
choices.B: To explain coordinated resistance to religious suppression and forced labor
choices.C: To encourage Pueblo communities to abandon their traditions voluntarily
choices.D: To praise colonial labor demands as just

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain coordinated resistance to religious suppression and forced labor
questions.explanation: The testimony describes sacred objects burned, labor demands, messengers, cords, villages, and coordinated revolt.

questions.prompt: Originalized Pueblo testimony, New Mexico, c. 1680: “The friars burned our sacred things and ordered us to labor for their churches. Messengers carried knotted cords between villages so that we would rise together and drive out those who mocked our ancestors.” Which phrase best supports the claim that colonial rule attacked Indigenous religious life?

choices.A: “Ordered us to labor”
choices.B: “Between villages”
choices.C: “Burned our sacred things”
choices.D: “Knotted cords”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Burned our sacred things”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows attacks on Indigenous religious practices.

questions.prompt: Originalized Pueblo testimony, New Mexico, c. 1680: “The friars burned our sacred things and ordered us to labor for their churches. Messengers carried knotted cords between villages so that we would rise together and drive out those who mocked our ancestors.” Which broader context best explains the uprising described?

choices.A: Indigenous communities in Spanish borderlands resisted missionary control, labor demands, and cultural suppression.
choices.B: Spanish colonial officials avoided missionary activity in North America.
choices.C: Pueblo communities lacked inter-village communication.
choices.D: The revolt occurred after the Cold War.

questions.correctAnswerText: Indigenous communities in Spanish borderlands resisted missionary control, labor demands, and cultural suppression.
questions.explanation: The source links friars, sacred objects, labor, village coordination, and resistance.

questions.prompt: Originalized Pueblo testimony, New Mexico, c. 1680: “The friars burned our sacred things and ordered us to labor for their churches. Messengers carried knotted cords between villages so that we would rise together and drive out those who mocked our ancestors.” Which statement best evaluates the testimony’s argument?

choices.A: It shows that Indigenous resistance could use local communication systems to coordinate action across communities.
choices.B: It proves that religious suppression never caused resistance.
choices.C: It claims that Spanish missionaries had no political authority.
choices.D: It argues that Pueblo communities accepted all colonial demands.

questions.correctAnswerText: It shows that Indigenous resistance could use local communication systems to coordinate action across communities.
questions.explanation: Messengers and knotted cords between villages indicate coordinated communication.

questions.prompt:
Table: Coffee’s transoceanic spread, simplified pattern
Region | Approximate development
Yemen, c. 1500s | Coffee cultivation and export through Mocha
Ottoman cities, c. 1500s–1600s | Coffeehouses spread as urban social spaces
Caribbean and Latin America, c. 1700s | Plantation coffee production expands under European empires
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Coffee moved from regional cultivation into wider imperial and consumer networks.
choices.B: Coffee cultivation began in the Caribbean before appearing in the Middle East.
choices.C: Coffeehouses ended urban sociability in Ottoman cities.
choices.D: European empires avoided plantation coffee production.

questions.correctAnswerText: Coffee moved from regional cultivation into wider imperial and consumer networks.
questions.explanation: The table traces coffee from Yemen through Ottoman cities to plantation production under European empires.

questions.prompt:
Table: Coffee’s transoceanic spread, simplified pattern
Region | Approximate development
Yemen, c. 1500s | Coffee cultivation and export through Mocha
Ottoman cities, c. 1500s–1600s | Coffeehouses spread as urban social spaces
Caribbean and Latin America, c. 1700s | Plantation coffee production expands under European empires
Which evidence best supports the claim that coffee shaped urban culture?

choices.A: “Export through Mocha”
choices.B: “Coffeehouses spread as urban social spaces”
choices.C: “Plantation coffee production”
choices.D: “European empires”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Coffeehouses spread as urban social spaces”
questions.explanation: This evidence directly connects coffee consumption to urban social life.

questions.prompt: Described coffeehouse scene, Istanbul, c. 1600: “Men sit on low benches drinking coffee, listening to a storyteller, and reading news sheets. A merchant points to sacks labeled ‘Mocha,’ while a religious official watches the gathering with concern.” Which interpretation is best supported by the scene?

choices.A: Coffee consumption created new social spaces that linked commerce, news, entertainment, and moral debate.
choices.B: Coffeehouses were isolated from trade networks.
choices.C: Ottoman cities rejected all imported commodities.
choices.D: Coffee drinking eliminated public discussion.

questions.correctAnswerText: Coffee consumption created new social spaces that linked commerce, news, entertainment, and moral debate.
questions.explanation: The scene includes coffee drinking, storytelling, news sheets, Mocha sacks, merchants, and a concerned religious official.

questions.prompt: Originalized Donghak proclamation, Korea, 1894: “Officials sell justice, foreign goods enter while peasants lose land, and the people bow beneath taxes. The Eastern Learning teaches that all persons carry Heaven within them; therefore corrupt rule must be corrected.” What was the most likely purpose of the proclamation?

choices.A: To defend corrupt local officials
choices.B: To mobilize peasant protest using religious and social reform language
choices.C: To promote European colonial rule in Korea
choices.D: To argue that taxation had no political importance

questions.correctAnswerText: To mobilize peasant protest using religious and social reform language
questions.explanation: The proclamation criticizes officials, taxes, foreign pressure, peasant land loss, and invokes Eastern Learning.

questions.prompt: Originalized Donghak proclamation, Korea, 1894: “Officials sell justice, foreign goods enter while peasants lose land, and the people bow beneath taxes. The Eastern Learning teaches that all persons carry Heaven within them; therefore corrupt rule must be corrected.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the movement challenged social inequality?

choices.A: “Foreign goods enter”
choices.B: “All persons carry Heaven within them”
choices.C: “Officials sell justice”
choices.D: “Eastern Learning”

questions.correctAnswerText: “All persons carry Heaven within them”
questions.explanation: This phrase expresses spiritual equality and challenges hierarchical social assumptions.

questions.prompt: Originalized Donghak proclamation, Korea, 1894: “Officials sell justice, foreign goods enter while peasants lose land, and the people bow beneath taxes. The Eastern Learning teaches that all persons carry Heaven within them; therefore corrupt rule must be corrected.” Which broader context best explains the grievances in the proclamation?

choices.A: Peasant unrest in late nineteenth-century Korea was shaped by corruption, taxation, social tension, and foreign pressure.
choices.B: Korea had no contact with foreign goods before 1900.
choices.C: Donghak rejected all criticism of government officials.
choices.D: Korean peasants had full political equality under industrial capitalism.

questions.correctAnswerText: Peasant unrest in late nineteenth-century Korea was shaped by corruption, taxation, social tension, and foreign pressure.
questions.explanation: The proclamation names corrupt officials, foreign goods, lost land, and taxes.

questions.prompt: Originalized Donghak proclamation, Korea, 1894: “Officials sell justice, foreign goods enter while peasants lose land, and the people bow beneath taxes. The Eastern Learning teaches that all persons carry Heaven within them; therefore corrupt rule must be corrected.” Which statement best evaluates the proclamation’s argument?

choices.A: It uses religious equality to justify criticism of corrupt government and social injustice.
choices.B: It separates religious language from social protest entirely.
choices.C: It claims that foreign pressure improved peasant life.
choices.D: It defends taxation as proof of fair government.

questions.correctAnswerText: It uses religious equality to justify criticism of corrupt government and social injustice.
questions.explanation: The source links Eastern Learning, equality, corruption, taxation, land loss, and social protest.

questions.prompt: Originalized Meiji reform statement, Japan, c. 1870s: “The country must learn from the world without losing the throne. Let conscripts serve the nation, schools teach useful knowledge, and officials survey land so taxes may be paid in money rather than old village obligations.” What was the most likely purpose of the statement?

choices.A: To promote selective modernization under imperial authority
choices.B: To abolish schooling and conscription in Japan
choices.C: To restore Tokugawa isolation without reform
choices.D: To make Japan a formal colony of European states

questions.correctAnswerText: To promote selective modernization under imperial authority
questions.explanation: The statement supports learning from the world, conscription, schooling, surveys, money taxes, and the throne.

questions.prompt: Originalized Meiji reform statement, Japan, c. 1870s: “The country must learn from the world without losing the throne. Let conscripts serve the nation, schools teach useful knowledge, and officials survey land so taxes may be paid in money rather than old village obligations.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the reformers valued practical education?

choices.A: “Losing the throne”
choices.B: “Schools teach useful knowledge”
choices.C: “Old village obligations”
choices.D: “Conscripts serve”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Schools teach useful knowledge”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows the value placed on practical education.

questions.prompt: Originalized Meiji reform statement, Japan, c. 1870s: “The country must learn from the world without losing the throne. Let conscripts serve the nation, schools teach useful knowledge, and officials survey land so taxes may be paid in money rather than old village obligations.” The reference to land surveys and money taxes most directly reflects which development?

choices.A: The strengthening of centralized fiscal systems to support modernization
choices.B: The end of state involvement in revenue collection
choices.C: The replacement of money taxes by tribute in kind
choices.D: The abandonment of national armies

questions.correctAnswerText: The strengthening of centralized fiscal systems to support modernization
questions.explanation: Land surveys and money taxes helped centralize revenue collection.

questions.prompt: Originalized Meiji reform statement, Japan, c. 1870s: “The country must learn from the world without losing the throne. Let conscripts serve the nation, schools teach useful knowledge, and officials survey land so taxes may be paid in money rather than old village obligations.” Which statement best evaluates the reform program described?

choices.A: It rejected foreign knowledge because it threatened imperial authority.
choices.B: It combined state centralization, mass education, military service, and fiscal reform.
choices.C: It preserved all village obligations without change.
choices.D: It separated modernization from government power.

questions.correctAnswerText: It combined state centralization, mass education, military service, and fiscal reform.
questions.explanation: The statement includes schools, conscription, land surveys, taxes, and imperial authority.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Samoa, c. 1880–1900, shows copra-producing coastal districts, German trading firms, United States and British naval stations, and treaty ports where chiefs negotiated under foreign pressure.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Pacific island economies and politics could become entangled with imperial commercial and naval rivalry.
choices.B: Samoa was isolated from global commerce after 1880.
choices.C: Copra production eliminated foreign interest in Pacific islands.
choices.D: Naval stations had no relationship to imperial competition.

questions.correctAnswerText: Pacific island economies and politics could become entangled with imperial commercial and naval rivalry.
questions.explanation: The map shows copra districts, foreign trading firms, naval stations, treaty ports, and chiefs negotiating under pressure.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Samoa, c. 1880–1900, shows copra-producing coastal districts, German trading firms, United States and British naval stations, and treaty ports where chiefs negotiated under foreign pressure.” Which feature best supports the claim that local rulers retained some agency despite imperial pressure?

choices.A: “Copra-producing coastal districts”
choices.B: “Chiefs negotiated under foreign pressure”
choices.C: “German trading firms”
choices.D: “Naval stations”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Chiefs negotiated under foreign pressure”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows local leaders acting within constrained imperial conditions.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Samoa, c. 1880–1900, shows copra-producing coastal districts, German trading firms, United States and British naval stations, and treaty ports where chiefs negotiated under foreign pressure.” Which broader context best explains the importance of naval stations?

choices.A: Industrial-era empires sought coaling, repair, and strategic stations to support steam-powered naval reach.
choices.B: Naval stations were unnecessary after steamships appeared.
choices.C: Pacific islands had no strategic importance before air travel.
choices.D: Naval power declined completely in the nineteenth century.

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial-era empires sought coaling, repair, and strategic stations to support steam-powered naval reach.
questions.explanation: Naval stations were valuable for steamship-era military and commercial power projection.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Samoa, c. 1880–1900, shows copra-producing coastal districts, German trading firms, United States and British naval stations, and treaty ports where chiefs negotiated under foreign pressure.” Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Imperialism in Oceania could combine commodity exports, naval strategy, and diplomatic pressure on local leaders.
choices.B: Pacific island chiefs had no role in treaty politics.
choices.C: Industrial-era imperialism avoided maritime strategy.
choices.D: Copra production was unrelated to global markets.

questions.correctAnswerText: Imperialism in Oceania could combine commodity exports, naval strategy, and diplomatic pressure on local leaders.
questions.explanation: Copra, firms, naval stations, treaty ports, and chiefs under pressure all support this argument.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, c. 1895: “A lecturer labeled ‘Science’ measures skulls while a businessman labeled ‘Empire’ points toward a map of Africa. A caption reads, ‘Nature has chosen the rulers.’” Which interpretation is best supported by the cartoon?

choices.A: Social Darwinist ideas were used to give pseudo-scientific justification to imperial hierarchy.
choices.B: European imperialists rejected racial theories.
choices.C: The cartoon supports anti-imperial equality without irony.
choices.D: Scientific racism had no relationship to empire.

questions.correctAnswerText: Social Darwinist ideas were used to give pseudo-scientific justification to imperial hierarchy.
questions.explanation: Skull measurement, “Science,” “Empire,” Africa, and the caption connect racialized science to imperial ideology.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, c. 1895: “A lecturer labeled ‘Science’ measures skulls while a businessman labeled ‘Empire’ points toward a map of Africa. A caption reads, ‘Nature has chosen the rulers.’” Which detail best supports the claim that the cartoon criticizes racialized science?

choices.A: “Businessman”
choices.B: “A map of Africa”
choices.C: “Measures skulls”
choices.D: “Lecturer”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Measures skulls”
questions.explanation: Skull measurement directly represents racialized pseudo-science.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, c. 1895: “A lecturer labeled ‘Science’ measures skulls while a businessman labeled ‘Empire’ points toward a map of Africa. A caption reads, ‘Nature has chosen the rulers.’” Which broader context best explains the cartoon?

choices.A: Nineteenth-century imperialism was often justified through racial theories and claims of scientific superiority.
choices.B: European empires abandoned racial ideology after 1800.
choices.C: Social Darwinism was limited to medieval religious debates.
choices.D: African colonization occurred without ideological justification.

questions.correctAnswerText: Nineteenth-century imperialism was often justified through racial theories and claims of scientific superiority.
questions.explanation: The cartoon reflects social Darwinist and scientific-racist justifications for empire.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, c. 1895: “A lecturer labeled ‘Science’ measures skulls while a businessman labeled ‘Empire’ points toward a map of Africa. A caption reads, ‘Nature has chosen the rulers.’” Which statement best evaluates the cartoon’s argument?

choices.A: It shows how economic and imperial interests could cloak inequality in the language of science.
choices.B: It proves that imperial rule was based only on humanitarian motives.
choices.C: It argues that racial hierarchy was rejected by all imperial states.
choices.D: It separates business interests from imperial expansion entirely.

questions.correctAnswerText: It shows how economic and imperial interests could cloak inequality in the language of science.
questions.explanation: “Science,” the businessman, “Empire,” Africa, and “Nature has chosen the rulers” show ideology serving power.

questions.prompt:
Table: Soviet collectivization, simplified pattern
Policy or result | Reported effect, c. 1929–1933
Forced consolidation of farms | Peasant resistance and livestock slaughter
State grain procurement targets | Food shortages in several regions
Restrictions on movement | Famine conditions intensified
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: State-directed collectivization could produce coercion, resistance, and severe human consequences.
choices.B: Collectivization was entirely voluntary.
choices.C: Grain procurement targets ended food shortages.
choices.D: Soviet agricultural policy had no effect on rural communities.

questions.correctAnswerText: State-directed collectivization could produce coercion, resistance, and severe human consequences.
questions.explanation: The table shows forced consolidation, resistance, grain procurement, movement restrictions, and famine.

questions.prompt:
Table: Soviet collectivization, simplified pattern
Policy or result | Reported effect, c. 1929–1933
Forced consolidation of farms | Peasant resistance and livestock slaughter
State grain procurement targets | Food shortages in several regions
Restrictions on movement | Famine conditions intensified
Which evidence best supports the claim that state power constrained rural populations?

choices.A: “Food shortages”
choices.B: “Peasant resistance”
choices.C: “Restrictions on movement”
choices.D: “Livestock slaughter”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Restrictions on movement”
questions.explanation: Movement restrictions show direct state coercion of rural populations.

questions.prompt:
Table: Soviet collectivization, simplified pattern
Policy or result | Reported effect, c. 1929–1933
Forced consolidation of farms | Peasant resistance and livestock slaughter
State grain procurement targets | Food shortages in several regions
Restrictions on movement | Famine conditions intensified
Which statement best evaluates the pattern shown?

choices.A: It demonstrates how totalitarian economic planning could transform rural society through coercion.
choices.B: It shows that peasants fully controlled Soviet policy.
choices.C: It proves that collectivization reduced state authority.
choices.D: It argues that rural resistance ended before collectivization began.

questions.correctAnswerText: It demonstrates how totalitarian economic planning could transform rural society through coercion.
questions.explanation: Forced farms, procurement targets, restrictions, resistance, and famine demonstrate coercive transformation.

questions.prompt:
Table: Nuclear weapons and civilian destruction, simplified World War II pattern
Location | Reported effect, 1945
Hiroshima | Massive blast and fire damage; long-term radiation sickness
Nagasaki | Major destruction in industrial and residential districts
Postwar debates | Questions about strategic bombing, civilian casualties, and new weapons
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: World War II introduced weapons whose destructive effects reshaped debates about war and civilians.
choices.B: Nuclear weapons affected only military bases without civilian consequences.
choices.C: Radiation sickness was unrelated to atomic bombing.
choices.D: Strategic bombing disappeared before 1945.

questions.correctAnswerText: World War II introduced weapons whose destructive effects reshaped debates about war and civilians.
questions.explanation: The table mentions blast, fire, radiation sickness, civilian casualties, and strategic bombing debates.

questions.prompt: Described photograph, Japan, 1945: “A burned cityscape shows twisted streetcar rails, a collapsed school building, and a signboard listing missing family members. A medical worker stands beside people with bandaged arms and faces.” Which interpretation is best supported by the photograph?

choices.A: Modern total war blurred distinctions between battlefield and civilian space.
choices.B: Civilian cities were unaffected by World War II technologies.
choices.C: The photograph shows only military targets.
choices.D: Urban destruction had no relationship to wartime policy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Modern total war blurred distinctions between battlefield and civilian space.
questions.explanation: The photograph shows burned urban infrastructure, a school, missing families, medical workers, and wounded civilians.

questions.prompt: Originalized Kenyan nationalist statement, c. 1950s: “The land taken for settlers has left many without a future, while emergency laws call every demand for dignity a crime. A nation cannot be built on passbooks, detention camps, and silence.” What was the most likely purpose of the statement?

choices.A: To defend settler control and emergency rule
choices.B: To criticize colonial repression and demand political rights
choices.C: To argue that detention camps created equality
choices.D: To oppose all forms of anticolonial organization

questions.correctAnswerText: To criticize colonial repression and demand political rights
questions.explanation: The statement criticizes settler land control, emergency laws, passbooks, detention camps, and silence.

questions.prompt: Originalized Kenyan nationalist statement, c. 1950s: “The land taken for settlers has left many without a future, while emergency laws call every demand for dignity a crime. A nation cannot be built on passbooks, detention camps, and silence.” Which phrase best supports the claim that colonial rule used coercive control?

choices.A: “Demand for dignity”
choices.B: “Passbooks, detention camps, and silence”
choices.C: “A nation”
choices.D: “Land taken”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Passbooks, detention camps, and silence”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows mechanisms of coercive colonial control.

questions.prompt: Originalized Kenyan nationalist statement, c. 1950s: “The land taken for settlers has left many without a future, while emergency laws call every demand for dignity a crime. A nation cannot be built on passbooks, detention camps, and silence.” Which broader context best explains the statement?

choices.A: African anticolonial movements after World War II challenged settler privilege, emergency rule, and racial hierarchy.
choices.B: European colonies in Africa all became independent without conflict before 1945.
choices.C: Colonial governments abandoned land control in the nineteenth century.
choices.D: Kenyan nationalism was unrelated to rural grievances or state repression.

questions.correctAnswerText: African anticolonial movements after World War II challenged settler privilege, emergency rule, and racial hierarchy.
questions.explanation: The statement fits postwar anticolonial challenges to land alienation and emergency repression.

questions.prompt:
Table: Nuclear arms control, simplified Cold War pattern
Period | Development

1968 | Treaty framework seeks to limit spread of nuclear weapons
1970s | Arms limitation talks attempt to manage superpower arsenals
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Nuclear technology created diplomatic efforts to manage proliferation and superpower rivalry.
choices.B: Nuclear arms control ended all Cold War competition.
choices.C: Non-nuclear states were absent from proliferation debates.
choices.D: Nuclear weapons had no influence on international diplomacy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Nuclear technology created diplomatic efforts to manage proliferation and superpower rivalry.
questions.explanation: The table shows proliferation debates, a treaty framework, and arms limitation talks.

questions.prompt:
Table: Nuclear arms control, simplified Cold War pattern
Period | Development
1960s | Nuclear-armed states and non-nuclear states debate proliferation risks
1968 | Treaty framework seeks to limit spread of nuclear weapons
1970s | Arms limitation talks attempt to manage superpower arsenals
Which statement best evaluates the pattern shown?

choices.A: Cold War diplomacy sometimes sought to regulate technologies that could threaten global survival.
choices.B: Nuclear diplomacy occurred only after the Cold War ended.
choices.C: Arms limitation talks eliminated distrust between superpowers.
choices.D: Treaties made nuclear weapons irrelevant to world politics.

questions.correctAnswerText: Cold War diplomacy sometimes sought to regulate technologies that could threaten global survival.
questions.explanation: Nuclear weapons created risks that treaty frameworks and arms talks attempted to manage.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: Late twentieth- and early twenty-first-century globalization included rapid urban growth in many formerly colonized regions.
choices.B: Megacity growth occurred only in Europe and North America.
choices.C: Urban populations declined in all listed cities.
choices.D: Globalization ended rural-to-urban migration.

questions.correctAnswerText: Late twentieth- and early twenty-first-century globalization included rapid urban growth in many formerly colonized regions.

questions.prompt:

questions.prompt: Originalized urban NGO report, c. 2020: “In the floodplain settlement, garment workers and street vendors lose wages when roads disappear under water. Families do not ask for charity alone; they ask for drainage, buses, secure tenure, and a place in city planning.” What was the most likely purpose of the report?

choices.A: To argue that rapid urbanization requires infrastructure, labor protection, and participatory planning
choices.B: To deny that flooding affects urban workers
choices.C: To promote the removal of all urban residents from cities
choices.D: To claim that informal workers are unrelated to globalization

questions.correctAnswerText: To argue that rapid urbanization requires infrastructure, labor protection, and participatory planning
questions.explanation: The report calls for drainage, buses, secure tenure, and inclusion in city planning.

questions.prompt: Originalized urban NGO report, c. 2020: “In the floodplain settlement, garment workers and street vendors lose wages when roads disappear under water. Families do not ask for charity alone; they ask for drainage, buses, secure tenure, and a place in city planning.” Which phrase best supports the claim that environmental risk affected economic life?

choices.A: “A place in city planning”
choices.B: “Garment workers and street vendors lose wages when roads disappear under water”
choices.C: “Secure tenure”
choices.D: “Families do not ask”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Garment workers and street vendors lose wages when roads disappear under water”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly links flooding to economic losses for workers.

questions.prompt: Originalized urban NGO report, c. 2020: “In the floodplain settlement, garment workers and street vendors lose wages when roads disappear under water. Families do not ask for charity alone; they ask for drainage, buses, secure tenure, and a place in city planning.” Which statement best evaluates the report’s argument?

choices.A: It presents urban poverty as a problem solved only by private charity.
choices.B: It links climate vulnerability, informal labor, infrastructure, and political inclusion in rapidly growing cities.
choices.C: It argues that city planning should exclude poor residents.
choices.D: It treats environmental risk as unrelated to governance.

questions.correctAnswerText: It links climate vulnerability, informal labor, infrastructure, and political inclusion in rapidly growing cities.
questions.explanation: The report connects flood risk, workers, roads, drainage, buses, tenure, and city planning.
